commentBody: That felt just like a fine Saturday puzzle during the solve, but it was over too soon.

Hi Caitlin,
If you don't know the 47A author, one of the Down entries is a problem, since the cross letter could be an A or an I.
Okay. I got it done pretty quickly but still enjoyed it. It made my brain work before going to sleep. NE corner fell last. Thanks.
Note on 53-Down (NOTE): What English-speaking musicians call B-natural is what their German counterparts call the key of H; see, for instance, Bach’s Messe in H-moll (Mass in B minor, BWV 232).

A very easy Saturday puzzle for me. I completed it 46.6% faster than my average Saturday. It was a clean solve for me, but I had trepidation in one spot, where 47A crossed 34D. The author's name, which I had to guess at, was not an inferable name; in fact, I'd never come across it before. And even though we just had "Mystic River" in a puzzle just the other day (was it the Midi?), I had no idea of anything about it, least of all, the author. All I knew is that it looked like a vowel had to be the crossing letter. 

But ET ALI- could end in an A or an I. Equal chance. 50/50. I felt the A was a better guess, and as it turned out, it was right.

I don't recall having to make a blind guess in a puzzle in forever. But there it was. I predict that this spot will rightly be called out as a Natick for the next 24--well, 20--hours.  (Sunday puzzles come out earlier.)

I did like Crystal and Rock for OSCAR HOSTS, and crossing MICA, at that.  ATTA was not an Indian flour today, and was back to the old-fashioned usage. MEWL is back; maybe today, some of the other day's complainers will realize it's a real word. OREO has a new clue and is next to PBJ.  The rocket to the moon SPACE SHOT was apropos today. 

Fun fact:  In German notation, H is indeed a NOTE.  (Equal to I flat, I think...) 

HELIO is in the dead center. I guess it's HELIOcentric today (as it always is).

The ELS weren't dead center in "fulfillment," though. 

But CECE was next to DEEJAY!

I put myself behind the eight ball on this one by trying marble ryes instead of JEWISHRYES and space ship instead of SPACESHOT and tamer instead of TAMED.  After asking (like Marvin GAYE) “What’s going on?”, I sorted it all out with a little thought and some crosses.  Of all the great soul singers, Marvin is my favorite, and I mourn all the great music we lost with his untimely death.

PBJS, OREOs, JEWISHRYES, MISO, EGGS; that’s two nights in a row with a plethora of edible entries.

A nice puzzle that had me at POMPOUSASS.
I liked the Saturday Mini, which, as a 7x7 grid, was a Maxi-Mini! Was this the first Mini that wasn't symmetric?  I don't remember seeing that before for the Minis.
SUPERSHOT and not spaceship was kinda rude
For the record, Alta doesn’t specifically ban snowboarding. They simply don’t allow snowboarders to use the chairlifts.

I very much enjoyed the puzzle, albeit it was a much quicker solve than I would expect. Thank you, Mr. Ferguson! And now I'm very much enjoying and groovin' to some Marvin GAYE and his magnificent, albeit heart-wrenching, "Inner City Blues"... https://youtu.be/57Ykv1D0qEE?si=jVLzkiUcmQnHdrlq
1A, well, never mind.  So hoped 14D would be Artemis II but I knew better.  Oh the wonderful BETATRON, so happy to see that.  Filled in collider first.

Was on the constructor's wavelength for this one. A faster than usual Saturday, and maybe, like Barry said, a fast Friday. It was fun, though!

Thanks, Royce,

Copernicus would like this one.

Go Gunners! 🏆🏆🏆

Fulfillment has 11 letters, therefore, the middle is ell, not els!
I am not fulfilled.

My favorite puzzle since Katie Hoody's

I didn't find it as easy as some others did; a pleasant challenge for me. Enjoyed all the clues/entries, even for OREO

And here I thought this puzzle was bound to be one that would be hard enough for the forum.  Apparently not so.  

It certainly took me, personally, long enough, assuming the time it gave me is correct.

I had to overcome GLamorRATTI, which now that I look at it doesn't look like it's even spelled right.  

Also I had noTED instead of CITED, GOTmarriED instead of GOTENGAGED, AnYA instead of ARYA, and tencommandments instead of GOLDENRULE.

I'm lying about the last one.  But I was glad to see GOLDEN RULE mentioned as a code for living in the puzzle, even if the church itself doesn't seem to think so, given all the effort going into getting the Ten Commandments posted in schools.  I would heartily endorse  the Golden Rule posted in schools, even though I am a doubter.

Two tennis clues. And on a day I spent ten hours in a tennis stadium.

TALIB/BETATRON was a Natick for me. Taliban was the obvious group there, but I didn't know the denonym for a member of the Taliban. That B was entirely a guess

29 Down EMAIL SCAM vs EMAIL SPAM

The C works equally well as the P for the 56 Across clue, ‘Gone’

CAST vs PAST

V annoying

I came here to mention that H is a musical note in German nomenclature, only to find I was late to the party. So I'll just add that H (= B natural) allows the creation of the familiar BACH motif in classical music. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/BACH_motif
YESINDEEDY this was a fun one, the SE was tough to crack for me. I'm sure this forum will love POMPOUSASS

Nice Saturday. 
I am sure folks will find it easier Saturday, but I don’t think we will get the “it was a nice Wednesday”

GLamouRATI really messed me up for a while, even though I had EMAILSPAM. 

My mind can not parse AT ALL.  
I can only see A TALL. 

Thank you for the Friday night diversion.

This was quite doable in the NW, NE, and SW quadrants. Solving the SE quadrant though took me as long as the rest of the puzzle.

Starting with SILENT DONOR did not help, especially as that second N crossed with the correct entry of NOTE. Of course I know no gospel singers, and even though I'm familiar with GAYE, the clue confused me. I've never seen "genie" pluralized as GENII - it's always been "genies" or just "jinn/djinn" for mme. There was also a sport clue... What 9n earth is a BETATRON?

In the end I dealt with it all without lookups, but it was super hard and I'm proud of myself for persisting.

We've got DEEJAY, HITSONGS, and Marvin GAYE, the prince of Motown. I've never really understood the difference between all those genres: rock and roll, soul, Motown, pop, hip-hop, rhythm and blues, country, etc. I guess there's a bit of an overlap. But here's my suggestion for a fun number, in honor of the moon peeps. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ddLd0QRf7Vg

🎶🍾🎈OREO is back. 

Since civility is required and should be expected we can now use 4/4/26-1A as code in the comments when necessary. (One of those dates that works even in the backwards US format) 

I‘m wondering whether the setter‘s location close to CERN is connected to the BETATRON answer. 

Nice puzzle. Not hard rather interesting.

Oof.

This rocket surgeon Swede 
(who has not yet had any caffeine
cuz this entire city somehow wakes up at 9) 

thought all y’all were supposed
to run through Swedish numbers,

and add a letter (e) to make a name.

Um, no CC.
Just…no.

Added confusion was
PBJS / marble? RYE and I was feeling utterly muddled.
 
Hubby arrived with tea 
and the rest was mostly smooth 
with a couple sticky wickets,
which I love.

Have a lovely Saturday all!

This was a satisfying Saturday solve, with a few crunchy corners. That bottom right corner was the toughest for me, but I got there in the end through sheer dumb luck and total guesswork. Putting donor before SANTA didn’t help.

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
If for no other reason, a strong effort for its novel (to me, anyway) clueing of OREO.

There should be a separate contest at Crosswordcons for cleverest clueings of fallow fills.

Not to say there weren't many other things to like and the grid did take me > 30 minutes to solve after all and not <, so I liked that it wasn't too quick.

And MEWL got a callback.

But still too many pesky-easy clues which could have been amped up with ease.

Ah the vaunted Saturday grid... Wordsworth sums it up best for me:

There was a time when meadow, grove, and stream,
       The earth, and every common sight,
                          To me did seem
                      Apparelled in celestial light,
            The glory and the freshness of a dream.
It is not now as it hath been of yore;—
                      Turn wheresoe'er I may,
                          By night or day.
The things which I have seen I now can see no more...

Whither is fled the visionary gleam?
Where is it now, the glory and the dream?
Mammals greatly diversified during the Eocene epoch, but arose much earlier, during the late Triassic.
I’m curious to see the unedited clue for 11A.

56A square could be C or P. 
An EMAIL SCAM can be filtered.
Something CAST is gone.

i thought - this is really hard for a sunday.  but the fact it was a sunday made me persevere, no look-ups, because of course a sunday should be something i can handily do.  then i realized it's saturday!  i woke up way too early, and clearly i'm not fully awake!  good puzzle though.

Oh those babies, MEWLing again! And since we just saw Mystic River, it’s nice that the author gets a shoutout today. I loved the movie, but the book is even better. Dennis Lehane is one of my favorite contemporary authors.

I found the top right section tough to break into, since I didn’t know the Kitty Kelley book (I guessed “Diana”), I don’t remember the YUGO, and am unfamiliar with ALTA. I also had “Got a fiancé” instead of GOT ENGAGED, and “space ship” instead of SPACE SHOT. So for me, it was like a chewy Wednesday with a gristly Saturday corner. I guess it just depends on what you know (and what you don’t).

Moving along quickly, the SE brought me to a screeching halt.

I was so sure of SECRET donor, and happily, so far from the days of office-speak that ACTION sTEp seemed fine.  As soon as I hit the words "college football" at 55 D, my eyes must have gazed over, and I guess I stopped reading.  That was too bad, because had I continued, "America's Game" would have jogged something loose and even *I* would have known it had to be ARMY or navY.

That was all good news because until I hit that corner, I was ready to complain that this was a Wednesday puzzle.  Untangling that mess added enough time onto my solve, that it put it into Friday territory.

I liked the misdirects in the tone of the clues.  Where I was expecting something formal, the answers turned out to be more casual.  Caitlin mentioned GOLDEN RULE and GOT ENGAGED, and I'll add SECRET SANTA to that list.
I entered the Chevy NOVA instead of YUGO, and it reminded me of the importance of product naming, as a car named No Va did not market well in nearby Mexico. Cheers!

This was a good puzzle! I really enjoyed it. Thanks, Royce!
What I do not love, and please forgive me if y’all have discussed earlier - I haven’t posted lately, is the new way that I have to go back and forth between the puzzle and Wordplay. I think it stinks. And it causes me frustration on Friday, Saturday, and Sunday when I need the most help. That is all. Thank you for coming to my Ted Talk.
Happy Saturday!

I love the moment when I add a single letter (from a cross) to a long answer that’s been stymie-ing me, and suddenly my brain sees the answer. Today’s puzzle, with its 18 longs, brought those happy pings again and again.

I also love being stuck in an area – as I was in the SE today – going over the clues to the answers that are eluding me, again and again, waiting for that sweet moment when the answer to one flashes, then it does, and suddenly the whole section falls.  As the SE did, with a rush.

I liked uncovering DAYS END, which, like yesterday’s FRET NOT, is a phrase I never use, but has such a lovely lilting quality, that I want to try to use it. 

I liked the cross of PBJ and JEWISH RYE, because that sounds like an intriguing combination to try.

Sometimes I come into the box neutral, go through highs and lows, and by completion I feel like I had a genuine experience and that my day has been better for it.  This was one of those puzzles, and thank you, Royce, for making it!
I started out with PBJS..but had doubts, and found that other ryes that would fit are seeded, Polish, and kosher...but I came back to the J.
Brilliant. Tough but accessible.
I don't get the LET clue. Is it a tennis reference?

Here I was, thinking this was another Wednesday puzzle masquerading as a Saturday. Like many others, I did have a little bit of trouble in the SE, but SECRET SANTA was my first thought. So the rival game easily was ARMY rather than Navy. BETATRON and ACTION ITEM held me up for a bit, until I realized that the answer probably had to be TALIB, an unfortunate answer that should never see the light of day in a puzzle.

But what got me was the Natick that was commented on at the very beginning: LEHANE crossing with ALIA. Since I do it on paper, I don't have the luxury of choosing between the two, so alas, I chose the wrong one. I've never heard of the author, so E or I could have worked. The good part is that I don't care about streaks; I'm just mad at myself for not finishing the puzzle correctly, perfectionist that I am.

Please can we roll back this change that’s making the column open in the same app as the puzzle? It’s so annoying having to go back and forth instead of just having the column open in one app and the puzzle in another.
No rye bread today, it’s Passover

Given the probabilistic nature of particle physics, the name BETATRON suggests that the scientists may have a sense of humour - or are looking for another potential revenue stream.

Nice puzzle. As many have noted, the bottom seemed more likely to bite.

As always, my hat’s off to our cadre of puzzle creators for their ingenuity:  this was a happy, crunchy puzzle for me — and I loved the appearance of SPACESHOT, given the progress of Artemis II just now.

Y’all have a lovely day.  Be kind to yourselves and one another, and go have another look at the “blue marble” pictures coming back from Integrity.  They make me think all over again,,,.

🎉 I finally got a proper challenge with this one. I started with marble RYE before JEWISH, wear out instead of POOP out, I ThInk sO before I TRIED TO, SECRET doNor before SANTA, okaY before COPY, and AnYA before ARYA. Changing that n to R was my last correction to get the gold star ⭐️ The downs really helped the SE corner, so I didn’t struggle there like some others. My challenge was the NE because my initial wrong answers were so far off. A couple of answers I knew right away were Dennis LEHANE and YUGO. A friend got a YUGO in high school. My folks wouldn’t let me ride in it because they didn’t trust the safety of it 😆 YES, INDEEDY, this was a fun solve today. Thanks, Royce!
Why is it so hard to access the Midi? First, it's not even there, then it says, "This page no longer exists." Can someone help me?

Appropriate answer for me today - 43 across. No big deal. Puzzle find today - a Sunday from March 19, 1989 by Caroline G. Fitzgerald with the title: "Doin' the Twist." One clue and answer example: "Betty Botta" BITOBETTERBUTTERBUYER Some other theme answers: SEASHORESHELLSELLER PECKOFPEPPERSPICKER SOLDIERSSHIRTSSEWER SIGHINGSCISSORSSEIZER Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/PS?date=3/19/1989&g=94&d=A See you tomorrow. ....

Seemed like a fine Saturday puzzle, with quite a few excellent long entries. Solved it without help, in a bit less time than yesterday's puzzle (which took me about half an hour, though many commenters felt yesterday's was rather easy). Knowing EOCENE, JEANRENOIR and SERRA helped here, and the several proper nouns that were unknowns came pretty easily from the crosses.

This one didn't feel "too easy" to me, but I'm curious to learn how others felt.

Fun puzzle today. Good to see the football rivalry shoe drop after Thursday's [Holy war]. The timing was a bit off for 1-D, because National Peanut Butter and Jelly Day was two days ago. You can't deny it's a classic of American cuisine, maybe even the Rodney Dangerfield of the lunch menu. Give me a PBJ and a stack of OREOs and I'm a satisfied luncher.

I get that LEHANE was obscure for some, but it was definitely not a Natick: we would have needed another obscure proper noun crossing it to meet that definition.

I was stumped trying to remember when The Rock or anyone named Crystal hosted the Academy Awards until I came here, but it fit even without really knowing what I was talking about. Also vacillated a bit on the Chevy Vega before I saw YUGO. I actually knew somebody who had one, although it didn't even last as long as my Le Car.

This is a perfect Friday puzzle, in my opinion.
Unfortunately, it's Saturday.

I enjoyed the solve:  good fill, nice cluing for the more common fill, nice longer answers.  But...
The puzzle provided no real resistance (for this reasonably experienced solver.)  No searching for a gimme that would give me a toehold into an empty area of the grid.  No moment of (pleasant) frustration.  No wondering, "Gosh, will I be able to finish this one?" No a-ha moments.

These are the features of a true Saturday puzzle, for me.

So, thanks, Royce Ferguson.  Nice puzzle.
Just the wrong day.
And that's on the editors.
I happened to know some right off the bat, like JEAN RENOIR, which led to a much faster solve than my average.  Still, nowhere near my best time.
Enjoyed this for the most part, some good word play, some tough trivia. Couple of mild head scratchers.
Finished both the LATimes and NYTimes puzzles very quickly this morning, and if that means they're easy I'm definitely OK with that.

Do I hear more MEWLing about the (long-lived) vocabulary in the puzzle?  Obviously, the other day was meant to soften folks up for the repeat....

42A was the hardest entry for me, given that SPACESHIP made sense initially....  I even took part of the YUGO out briefly... And BTW, wasn't that the automobile that was touted because when it broke down, it could SERVE as a backpack?

Is it actually "disrespectful" to nail a self-important bloviator as a One-Across?  Asking for a friend....

My work here is done....

My comments got emu’d twice yesterday so here’s an abbreviated version:
I cheekily called out Sam as an editor for allowing JEWISH RYE to be an entree on Passover. 
I noted the timeliness of SPACESHOT and wished the astronauts well. I reminisced about the old silent film from 1902 “A Trip to the Moon” and included a link but you can Google it.
I praised the engaging MICA and GLITTERATI juxtaposition and the clever related clue for OSCAR HOSTS. I extolled Billy Crystal’s 1992 Oscars (Silence of the Lambs) as the best opening and hosting job ever.
I hoped that folks who missed MEWL the other day got  it today and thanked folks who thought the college war yesterday was ARMY and Navy since it reminded me today. I also noted that LET was a lot easier to get since I had tennis on the brain with BREAKSERVE.
Finally I said this was a fairly straightforward challenge today but had a lot of fun clues and a great grid, I enjoyed it. I think often the thing that would really make me happier is just to have fewer gimmes and more ambiguity in the shorter crosses; it wouldn’t take much to make this more of a Saturday for me.

Hope the comments make it through today, not sure what the problem was yesterday. I did reference yesterday’s clue for college h**y war maybe that was it?

Show of hands:

Exactly how many of us had "marble" before JEWISH?
This ballad, in good time, quite vividly came to mind. But not until I studied the grid as an explanatory abstract. https://youtu.be/QrCdgJ3teRg?si=od_fa6feWwmoooHG BON Jour, BRU

It's funny how I misremember pop culture info I don't know.  While I wouldn't know Winans if I fell over her, some vague signal went off in my brain that she was either DEDE or CECE and I didn't know which.  I wrote in the wrong one first, natch. I feel that bees have received more press coverage in the queen domain than ANTS have. QUEEN BEE is a popular expression; QUEEN ANT, not so much.  Nor was ARYA any help at all.  Some of you may know why I might be expected to know that -- but of course I didn't know it.  Nor any of the other GOT family members. Not hard for a Saturday, but I found this very enjoyable throughout.  Love the word GLITTERATI and the phrase USHER OUT.  Love the clue for OSCAR HOSTS.  Loved BREAK SERVE -- though you can also HOLD SERVE after ad in.  It just takes a few more points -- and that could have been indicated more clearly.  "Win immediately after ad in" would have been better. Re the popular loaves at 17A -- Here's one of the greatest TV ad campaigns of all time: https://fontsinuse.com/uses/14355/levy-s-ad-campaign-you-don-t-have-to-be-jewis#zoom-8

I manged ro power my way through this one.  MEWL threw me.  Never heard of a BETATRON.

On another note,  what's up with the mini Puzzle today?  I get GOLDENGATE, but the graphic doesn't make any sense.
I need to remember in the future, when there is a clue that I can't make out at all such as "win after ad out" I should think: Tennis.
Broke my streak with emailsCam and Cast vs email spam and past. alas.

Oh dear, frustration on my part on just not knowing so much. Sigh.   

We had a Mystic River clue recently and I looked it up so knew the author (or was that a different crossword? no-one else seems to remember it). 

Never heard of the bread nor the connection with corned beef. 

Didn't know the slogan for Oreos. I tried one once, very hard and not much taste.

Then SERRA, ARYA, EPA, ELS, SUPES, OPRAH, YUGO, ATTA, (must try to remember that one), Crystal and Rock, CECE, BETATRON, Kazaa and Wishmaster, ARMY. Very faintly remembered GOLDEN RULE from some US film.

Sorry to moan. If you knew all these, then it was easy!

Good morning! Slightly hung over - I had 2 lemon basil martinis last night and wow! They hit me like a ton of bricks! Probably the sugar + alcohol. I'm generally a wine drinker. 
Good Saturday challenge, and I'm relieved to see my brain is functional. 
(I was considering Crystal and Rock as some sort of "salts" until I copped on to the capital R. Anyone else?)
Anyway, I have no idea what my real time would have been, as I got up and folded a large load of wash right in the middle of it.
Off to bake and clean. Need to make rolls and a banana cake with dark chocolate frosting for Easter dinner.
Have a good Saturday
SW corner took me forever

Some claim the puzzles are trending less difficult than in the past.  My times this week support that theory to some degree.  Monday and Tuesday were right at average times, but Wednesday through Saturday times were 34%, 50%, 54%, and 45% faster than average, respectively.

Even though I got today's puzzle done quickly relative to my average, I still  enjoyed the solve, and it still took 41:51, which is a long time by some others' standards.  My Saturday average is 1:16:00.  

And I am not newbie solver so I don't believe I am getting significantly faster.  I have a 98% completion rate without lookups since I started solving online about seven years ago.
It’s Thursday again!

Here is what I thought, this was a nice Saturday puzzle. And then I thought about a comedy where everyone drives a Yugo with personalized plates. Drowning Mona, https://shorturl.at/pAON1 I thought it was funny, even though it didn't get great reviews.

Fun solve, good enjoyable clues. I managed to solve it fairly quickly for a Saturday (which for me is never a guarantee solve anyway without lookups). But it's nice to feel good about yourself.

I enjoyed the challenge of 43A. First went with I THINK SO then BELIEVE SO before finally unlocking I TRIED TO and that ended up being the last piece of the puzzle.

Some tricky stuff like ELS, MICA, SRO, MEWL, CECE. But - and this is the most important thing - the trivia-y stuff was never crossed together, which I find to be the most annoying thing. So Bravo, good puzzle, thoroughly enjoyed
The crossing of LEHANE and ALIA bugged me a bit, given the ambiguity of ALIA/ALIi and me not knowing who Dennis is. Otherwise nice and smooth, if not a little straightforward for a Saturday.

Meaty puzzle. I made 2 passes through the clues until I had enough coverage to work on specific areas. Nice touch of nostalgia with the 2D clue for an xworder's favorite cookie. I'm noticing more slang conversational answers like 62A. Do these phrases tick me off? YESINDEEDY.

As a Jew, I have always felt a bit odd about having a rye named after me. I don't take severe umbrage, it's not that big a deal, but it's a cultural oddity that is on the outskirts of offense -- or maybe not. What say you? It's more like "Eastern European Rye imported more by Ashkenazi Jews in the early part of the 20th Century,, but certainly not exclusively by that group," which I think would be a great way to list it on the deli menu. 
If you ever dunk an Oreo in anything, you and I are through. Just eat the thing. It doesn't get dunked. I will accept separating it into sections or licking off the white part, but heaven help you if you start dunking. In milk or anything else. We aren't animals.

Here’s to you, YUGO. You cost less than $5,000, were unlovely, broke down again and again, were called by Time magazine “the worst car ever”, and were so basic that it seemed that someone with a six-pack and a screwdriver could take you apart in an afternoon.

Yet, you never pretended to be more than you were, you were accessible, and you had the best of intentions. Your body had its faults, but your heart was impeccable.

You engendered laughs; you engendered warm smiles. You had fans and detractors. Your day in the sun was brief yet vivid.

Here’s to you, Yugo, the Little Car That Could … and did.

TALIB simply means student in Pashto. It's unfortunate that the militant group co-opted the word, as they only study one book...and it's not "Mystic River."

Go ARMY, beat Navy!
Thanks for this fine Saturday puzzle to do an a (very rare) wet Saturday in Austin.

Anyone else spend time hunting for the happy noise with SPACESHip instead of shot? Had never heard of a car, and I was thinking of many possibilities for LEP to be a realistic answer 😂 

My favorite answer was eggs as easily beaten. 

Did Billy Crystal do a good job hosting the Oscar’s?

Some days things just seem to click. I can't explain it. Today I seemed to be on Royce's wavelength. He had some top-notch misdirecting clues -- [Protectors of a queen] and [Crystal and Rock, notably], for example -- and some that were marginally obscure, like [The sparkle in some cosmetics].  It wasn't an easy puzzle, but it felt satisfying, all the way through. A good, chewy, fun one to solve.  Thank you!
Frustrating that the app won't simply link to the wordplay column so you can read

Enjoyed the puzzle, was stuck on BETATRON/TAMED for a while, not being familiar with the former, and reading wordplay where there wasn’t any into the latter (I had SATED until …RENOIR yielded the N for MEANIE. 

In Michigan, the YUGO was infamously known as the car that was blown off of the Mackinac Bridge by a gust of wind into the water below. 

Anyone else remember that rumor(?). 

I guess it’s time for me to “research” the topic.

I thought that was a fun, crunchy one! I finished up just about 2 minutes better than my average. Many Saturdays of late, I have finished well better than my average, like by 60-70% or more. So this was very satisfying for me even if it's still too easy for others. And I get it!

That said, and knowing that I'm a relative newbie, I am astonished at the talk of the crossing of LAHANE and ALIA  being a natick. I get it about the i verses A in ALIA, but I think of Lahane has very well known.  But I guess, we all just know what we know. Just really surprised me that he might be considered obscure.

However, I had no idea about the tennis clues, but they did come through with the crosses even if I didn't understand until I came out here.

I don't know what it is exactly, but twist, lick, and dunk gets a big ewww from me. I've never been a big Oreo fan anyhow, or any store-bought cookie,but if I knew that was the slogan, it sure would have turned me off.

Fun Saturday puzzle. I was stuck on space-ship for a long time until I said YUGI out loud & my husband corrected me. Team work makes the dream work, or so they say. I attribute any struggles I had to the shingles vaccine (2nd dose) I had yesterday. IYKYK. 🤒
Excellent Saturday. Just enough crunchy to be interesting but not frustrating!

I know there are lots of issues with folks with the app and things changing, but it's odd because I feel like my things have changed in different ways than what everyone else is reporting. 

Also, my app is wildly inconsistent with itself. It's like there's two versions of it that flip flop however it sees fit. 

Some things are consistent though, like I don't have access to the search button, like I do on my computer, and there are no avatars or profile pics showing. It's so weird!

I don't even know what to try anymore. I've restarted my phone, I've uninstalled the app several times and reinstalled it, restarting the phone again, etc. but nothing works...
I'm always surprised that proper nouns seem to be unpopular-- if not for answers I knew like JEANRENOIR and LEHANE, I would've given up early on this one.
SPACESHOT feels like crossword malpractice…
Was this an especially tricky Saturday or was I just having one of my "eighties senility" days?

Good Saturday puzzle, even if it took a while to do it.  Got myself "crossed up" by having SPACESHIP & YUGI on the East edge, which left LEP for an obvious entry.  Had a struggle with the Southeast section even though SECRETSANTA & JEANRENOIR fell early.  But it was fun!

Marvin GAYE got me distracted because it made me try yo think of another Motown Man....produced Thriller....married to Peggy Lipton....one of the music industry's most prolific and influential figures....Barry Gordy....darn....Jones! Quincy Jones! After that I  could get back to the puzzle....whew....

Here’s to you, YUGO.  You cost less than $5,000, were ugly, broke down again and again, were called by Time magazine “the worst car ever”, and were so basic that it seemed that someone with a six-pack and a screwdriver could take you apart in an afternoon.  

Yet, you never pretended to be more than you were, you were accessible, and you had the best of intentions.  Your body had its faults, but your heart was impeccable.

You engendered laughs; you engendered warm smiles.  You had fans and detractors.  Your day in the sun was brief yet vivid.  

Here’s to you, Yugo, the Little Car That Could …  and did.
Oscar hosts and Lehane was the worse combination ever
Not fair having Jewish rye during Passover week. And Oreo to boot. No indeedy!

Maybe I’m just getting stupider or something, but this one was not at all easier for me than most Saturdays. It took me 71% more time than my Saturday average. I’m not complaining, but I’m also not buying into the “easier than the good old days” thing.

Not a taxing puzzle at all but I found it very engaging. First, very not Kosher for Passover—JEWISH RYE? Shame on you, Sam Ezersky, you’re an editor and a nice Jewish boy like you should know better than serving leavened bread today. On the flip side “rocket to the moon” was very topical, good luck Artemis astronauts! Somehow I thought it might be a callout to this classic early silent film: https://m.youtube.com/watch?v=ZNAHcMMOHE8&pp=0gcJCcQBo7VqN5tD What else? Loved the MICA and GLITTERATI juxtaposition and then the equally apropos clue for OSCARHOSTS right next to it. (Billy Crystal—1992 —still the best Oscar opening ever). Hopefully if MEWL was new to you the other day it wasn’t today. Thanks to those who thought the college “Holy War” yesterday was ARMY/Navy, you put a bug in my ear today. And speaking of bug in my ear I loved the LET clue but got it a lot faster since I had tennis on the brain with BREAKSERVE. Yeah, so more of a Friday, I suppose, and I keep saying all it would take to make me happier is fewer gimmes and more ambiguity on the shorter clues. But still, a fun grid and I can’t say I didn’t enjoy myself.

I can live with spaceship, even got it after a while, but els cost me a fair bit. The center of fulfillment is an el, not both.

Yeah, yeah, clues, I know, but it still bugged me.

I guess all I can say is "you got me."

Regarding 53 Down, actually there is a note called H.  In some German-speaking countries, B natural is called H.

H *is* a note in German and quite frankly it is shameful that a puzzle appearing in the New York Times has such an error.  It is a poor and misleading clue.  The editor should have caught that and disqualified the puzzle from being published.

52 down: In the older European system H is one of the notes of the scale, equivalent to B natural.

@The X-Phile
Generally speaking, a good plan. But even on paper, in ink, if I'm looking at blanks the second time through, I tend to put an S, EST, or ER at the end of the answer if the clue is - respectively - a plural, a superlative or a comparator.  (It's right often enough that I accept that I might have to make a messy change later).

Today, I took "Less likely to bite..." as a comparator, and went with the "er" suffix, to my detriment. But I can't complain - it's a perfectly fair clue!

BTW, I'm less often tempted to put "ed" at the end when the clue is in the past tense. Sometimes it pays off, but English has a lot of irregularities in temporal tenses. Think sit/sat, run/ran, say/said, swim/swam, etc. And today, although 13 down was past tense, and did happen to end in ED, the fact that it was 10 letters long told me that it was likely more than one word, and it's not always the last word in a phrase that carries the tense. (At first. I wanted "said yes to", but of course it doesn't fit). Anyway, I wasn't tempted to fill in the ED early, even though that would've helped with TAMED.

@ThunderYes, a let is called when the serve lands in the service box (hence not a fault), but hits the net en route. You get a do over.
Yes, it is a tennis reference. A LET serve results in a do-over without penalty; it does not result in a "fault" (and if the server double-faults they lose the point).
@Bob Yep. I think BREAKSERVE is too.

@Bob

Some specifics: If a serve lands in the service box, it must be returned. If it lands outside the service box, it’s a fault. If the serve clips the net, and lands in the service box, it is a do-over without penalty. In this case, it’s called a LET.

@Bob
Yes.  It's not a Fault, but the serve must be replayed.

No Katie,
The original Natick had two proper nouns, but Rex Parker, who called the first Natick, broadened the meaning years ago to mean the crossing of two answers neither of which most solvers would be expected to know. No solver can know from [Et] when it is ALIA or ALII, so it comes down to whether "most solvers" are expected to know whether LEH_NE takes an A or an I.
There is a separate discussion of whether two proper nouns should ever cross, but that is not a Natick discussion. Our David Connell called that an NxN (spoken as Nixon).

@Mean Old Lady Yugos were famous for pretty much falling apart after 12,000 miles or so.

@SP Do you understand the LET? still don't get that but seems to be understood by most others.

@slowlearner

If you don’t get your serve in that’s called a fault. (Losing a point because you don’t get both serves in is called a double fault.) A let is a serve that hits the net and then goes in—it’s basically a do-over, it doesn’t count. So it’s nobody’s “fault”.

@slowlearner

It’s another tennis term. A let is sort of a foul that stops play without penalty (or fault, in tennis parlance). In my experience, this is most common when the
Serving player strikes the net with the ball, but the ball continues into the opponent’s court. 

If the ball hits the net and drops to the server’s side, or goes out of bounds, that would be a *fault*.

@Lewis For some behind-the-Iron-Curtain context: In out part of the world the Yugo was considered a premium car before 1989 🤣 And no wonder! Most people didn't own a car, and the most popular model *for families with kids* was this: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fiat_126 (You had to wait 5 or 10 years to get one of those)
@Andrzej -- I think the same company (Zastava) made both the Fiat 126 and the Yugo.

FSM (Fabryka Samochodów Małolitrażowych, literally the Small Displacement Car Plant) made the Polish Fiat 126p, using an Italian design from the 60s, well into the 90s. @HeathieJ I love how some are surprised that others think Lehane is obscure. I don't know that name from Adam. I know very little of modern fiction. It just doesn't interest me.

Our own esteemed crossword columnist herself admitted she didn't know Dragnet. I was very surprised about that. There's a 1987 film. A TV show that is in constant reruns. And it was a ground breaking, trend setting radio show. The whole franchise along with spinoffs is still an influence on procedurals and hospital dramas. Jack Webb's voice and mannerisms are still recycled in comedy and cartoons. Yet she had to look up Dragnet.
We know what we know and shouldn't be surprised at what others don't.

In tennis, a LET serve is a do-over, so neither player would get a fault.

@MWC Reasonable, but the columnist is not the programmer. While you may get eventual acknowledgement via the columnist forwarding your request to Support so it can be forwarded to the correct team such that Dev can eventually see it, it would likely be more efficient to send a message to Support directly, and preferably with screenshots and/or a very specific description of where you were in the app and what button you hit that opened up the column in the app, including your OS version and model of phone. Might seem like it doesn’t matter, but it does.

(I do recognize it this might seem redundant, especially if you are reporting a thing specifically in regards to Wordplay or the Crossword, but I promise you, as IT/dev, it’s way easier to have that information directly in the ticket so we don’t have to go hunt for it. It’s one of those things that will get a request answered way faster than another without that information. Even if it seems like it should be simple to get.)

@Kathy Celer
In tennis, when the non-serving player wins a game, they are said to have “broken serve”. The potentially game determining point is either “ad in”, if the server has the advantage, or “ad out” if the game would go to the receiver, if they win that point. So if the receiver wins the point at ad out, they “break serve”, winning the game. If the server wins the point, the game reverts to deuce and continues. A game is only won on an ad(vantage) point.
